L01 Introduction

Data Visualization (STAT 302)

Author

INSTRUCTIONS

TipGithub Repo Link

A link to your GitHub repo should be placed at the top your submitted html.

Goals

  1. Ensure software for this course is properly installed and functional
  2. Understand the importance of visualization
  3. Develop and follow a proper workflow
  4. Construct and explore a few plots using ggplot2
  5. Add layers to a plot that include title, axis, and pre-set themes

Don’t worry if you cannot do everything here by yourself. You are just getting started and the learning curve is steep, but remember that the instructional team and your classmates will be there to provide support. Persevere and put forth an honest effort and this course will payoff.

TipOrganization is important!

  1. All packages should always be loaded at the TOP of the document.

  2. All data should be in your data/ subdirectory. Data loading should go at the top of the document under the libraries.

Tasks

Complete the following tasks and include some indication that you have completed each in your document.

Task 1

Download and install R Software version 4.5.3.

  • If you’ve previously installed R with version < 4.5.3, uninstall the old version first.
  • Windows users: direct link
  • Mac users
    • Intel Mac: direct link
    • Apple Silicon (M1, M2, etc.): direct link

Task 2

  1. Download and install RStudio.
    • Windows users: direct link
    • Mac users: direct link
  2. Open RStudio and ensure it and R have been properly installed.
  3. Go to Tools > Global Options and change the two indicated settings below and click Apply. Your other options are likely different than what is pictured. This is okay and we suggest you leave them as is until you become more familiar with RStudio.

Task 3

Install the following packages:

  • tidyverse, actually it is a collection/universe of packages being installed
  • skimr
  • devtools

Easiest method: run the following code in RStudio’s console

install.packages(c("tidyverse", "skimr", "devtools"))

Task 4a

Create a GitHub account.

Task 4b

Install Git. If needed, see Chapter 6 of Happy Git and GitHub for the useR for a detailed instruction.

  • Windows users
    • Download and install Git for Windows
    • NOTE: When asked about “Adjusting your PATH environment”, make sure to select “Git from the command line and also from 3rd-party software”.
  • Mac users
    • Open the Terminal app on your computer
    • Run this command in Terminal: xcode-select --install
      • Accept the offer! Click on “Install”.

Once you successfully installed Git, double-check following 6.1 - Git already installed?.

Task 4c

Once Task 4a & 4b are done, we need to connect our local Git with GitHub, the remote Git server. To do so, we need to create a credential (personal access token or PAT) and have Git memorizes it.

In RStudio’s console, type and run

usethis::create_github_token()
  • Fill out the Note section
  • Set Expiration to Custom -> 07/01/2026
    • I don’t recommend using the “No expiration” option
    • Once your PAT expires, you need to redo Task 4c
  • No need to change the Scope section
  • Click “Generate token”
  • Copy the generated PAT to your clipboard. Or leave that browser window open and available for a little while, so you can come back to copy the PAT.

Go back to RStudio’s console. Type and run

gitcreds::gitcreds_set()

Paste the PAT in response to the dialogue in the console:

? Enter password or token: ghp_xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
-> Adding new credentials...
-> Removing credentials from cache...
-> Done.

Task 5

Since organization is essential for any data science project or work, we should begin by setting up an organizational structure for this course and its contents.

  1. Setup a directory/folder on your laptop that will contain all your materials and work for this course. You may already have one setup and named. We will setup this directory and call it stat-302. Where this is located on your laptop is completely up to you.

  2. Within stat-302/, place a copy of the course syllabus for future reference. We also will create a new sub-directory that will contain all labs for this course. For ease let us call it labs. So now we have stat-302/labs/.

  3. Open RStudio and create a “New Project” using “Version Control” -> “Git”

  4. Copy the URL from GitHub. Browse so that you place this project within the stat-302/labs/ folder that you created.

  5. Rename template_L00.qmd to your Lastname_Firstname_L00.qmd

  6. Render the file with Cmd/Ctrl + Shift + K or click the Render button.

Task 6

Take some time to explore the Quarto and ggplot2 websites. We suggest bookmarking them because they will be extremely useful references.

We highly recommend reading through the Tutorial: Hello, Quarto.

Task 7 (optional)

If you are new to RStudio we strongly recommend reading through Chapters 1-4 of the Stat 202 text book

Exercise 2

Let’s look at some interesting patterns in the history of LEGO! We’ll be using data taken from the lego package. A copy of the data is located here data/legosets.rda.

First, let’s look at the average cost of LEGO sets over time. The main variable of interest here is USD_MSRP, or the manufacturer’s suggested retail price in constant dollars. Let’s recreate the following graphic.

Exercise 4

Now you try, let’s look at how the number of pieces per set has changed over time. Because Duplo sets are much smaller (since they’re designed for toddlers), we made a special indicator variable for them in the code below.

pieces_per_year <- legosets |>
  # new indicator variable
  mutate(Duplo = ifelse(Theme == "Duplo", "Duplo", "Lego")) |>
  # group_by() both year and Duplo
  # summarize() mean pieces

Use an appropriate graph to plot the number of pieces over time. Have either separate lines or separate plots for the Duplo and Lego categories. Be sure the title, x-axis, and y-axis are properly labeled. Add a pre-set theme to make the background look nicer.

What trends do you notice in the plots? Are you surprised by the plots?

TipSolution

YOUR SOLUTION HERE
